data.title: Gray Media: A Value Play On A Highly Anticipated 2026 Midterm Election Season
data.description:
It's said that the stock market is forward-looking by around six months. If that is the case, value investors who also happen to have fairly high-risk tolerance should put Gray Media, Inc. (NYSE:GTN) on their radar right now. Gray Media is the largest owner of top-rated local television stations and digital assets in the United States. It serves 113 television markets that collectively reach approximately 37% of US television households. As one would expect with a rapidly changing media landscape, GTN's legacy television business has been on a slow, downward decline. As has its stock price since 2022. However, there was a brief period of time from October 2023 to January 2024 when the stock rose by more than 50%. As the United States was heading into a highly anticipated election season, political spending on advertising was top of mind. That instinct ended up being correct as GTN achieved a $3.36 EPS last year on the strength of its political ad revenue. An absurd EPS figure relative to today's stock price of around the same number.

GTN falling to this level is not completely without merit. Its expected weak 2025 performance and large debt burden are legitimate concerns. However, I believe that GTN management just needs to hold the fort during this trough, as it won't be too much longer before the 2026 midterm election season is upon us. Without getting too political, there are factions of people in the United States who are already looking forward to the midterm elections. I believe that we will see record levels of interest and engagement from the voting population for a midterm election. That high level of interest will result in a lot of political ad spending. Spending that will run straight to GTN's bottom line.

Reviewing GTN's Q4 2024 earnings release, we see a year-over-year 11% increase in revenue for the full year and a turn from a $1.39 per share loss in 2023 to a $3.36 EPS in 2024. This variance is all driven by political advertising, which was $497 million in 2024 compared to $79 million in 2023. Simply excluding political advertising is not a good way to compare year-over-year results because the jump in political advertising will cannibalize non-political ad revenue. The best way to compare growth is to look at 2022 to 2024 when political ad revenue is high for both years. The company is well aware of this and provides that data within its release:

A combination of essentially flat revenue and increasing costs from 2022 to 2024 shows that the business is challenged. Combine that with the fact that a good chunk of the $3.36 EPS seen in 2024 will be given back in losses in 2025 and the P/E ratio of around 1x makes more sense. Q1 2025 revenue guidance has a midpoint of $770 million, down 6.5% from Q1 2024 revenue of $823 million and down 3.9% from Q1 2023 revenue of $801 million.

GTN has a high debt level, and servicing it is cutting into its profitability. Interest expense was $485 million, $440 million, and $354 million for 2024, 2023, and 2022, respectively. Given flat-lining revenue, that trend cannot continue. The company has made headway in reducing its debt load. Long-term debt decreased from $6.1 billion to $5.6 million from the end of 2023 to the end of 2024. An additional $250 million in debt has been authorized to be bought back on the open market this year. The reduction in debt has led the company to project $450 million in interest expense for 2025.

From an operating standpoint, there is more bad than good, but the market may be punishing GTN a bit too much for it. GTN currently ranks as an A+ on Seeking Alpha's valuation grade. Though that grade might be a little overstated to reality.

GTN's GAAP P/E of 1x is obviously far below the sector median. However, due to the nature of one good year, one-off year, the forward P/E has no meaning and therefore no grade. Due to the high debt load compared to its market cap, EV/EBITDA multiples are a better measure of GTN's relative valuation. In GTN's "good" year, its multiple is about half of the sector median. In the "bad" year, it's about 15% too high. That would suggest to me that the stock price is undervalued by about 25%. GTN trades well below book value, but considering that a high proportion of its assets are intangibles or illiquid with a heavy debt load, I don't consider its discount to book to be a driving force of value. The final clincher in favor of GTN for me is its low Price to Cash Flow metric. Despite the business being stagnant and persistent challenges in navigating shifts in the way the public consumes media, the company generates cash today. Whether it's a good year or a bad year. The operating cash flow for the last three years was $751 million in 2024, $648 million in 2023 and $829 million in 2022. Enough to pay down debt. Enough to pay the quarterly $0.08 dividend, which currently yields nearly 10% at today's stock price.

Conclusion: On my watch list for a potential buy

Some companies are considered good businesses but with an expensive stock. Gray Media is the mirror opposite. The business isn't great, but it's very cheap. I'll give credit to management; it's doing what it needs to do to survive the down years while putting up big numbers during election years. Right now, the stock has no momentum, and election ad season might be a few months too far out even for a forward-looking market. So, there is no point in rushing in just yet. However, I think GTN's revenue potential as well as the hype around its revenue potential once election season nears will be larger than it has ever been. Time it just right and there is potential for a profitable investment in a short timeframe. Therefore, I am giving GTN a buy rating while adding it to my watchlist in preparation for a possible purchase in a few weeks or months.

data.title: Retire With A Stress-Free 9%-Yielding Monthly Paying Dividend Portfolio
data.description:
Retiring on dividends requires that you have not only a sufficient amount of current income to cover your living expenses, but also you need that income to be sustainable over the long term, while also growing at a rate that meets or beats inflation over time. You can take two approaches to growing your income over time: you can either generate it through dividend per share growth in your underlying holdings, or you can generate it through retaining some of your dividend income and reinvesting it to buy additional shares of dividend-paying stocks.

Of course, if you take the latter approach, you need to make sure that your dividend income comfortably exceeds your living expenses so that you can be sure to have extra capital on hand every month to buy additional shares. You need to make sure that you have the self-discipline necessary to not spend that money because if you do not, while you may not notice it in the short term, over the long term, prices are going to start rising, and your passive income is not going to rise along with it.

With that principle in view, in this article I am going to share how you can build a pretty simple yet well-diversified and sustainable stream of passive income that yields 9% on a weighted average basis, giving you as an investor plenty of disposable income to both meet your living expenses as well as grow your passive income to keep up with inflation over the long term. To do this, you can simply own three funds that will give you broad diversification across many sectors with a particularly heavy weighting in real asset-backed, recession-resistant sectors that can help your income hold up well during even fairly sharp economic downturns.

Portfolio Component #1

The first fund is the most diversified. It is the NEOS S&P 500 High Income ETF (SPYI). Effectively, it is an S&P 500 Index (SPY) fund that implements a notional covered call strategy that targets a 1% monthly yield from the options premiums generated. Additionally, by using Section 1256 contracts, SPYI enjoys tax benefits with about 60% of the options premium distributions being taxable at long-term rates and 40% at short-term rates, which is very favorable compared to the typical treatment of options premium income, which is typically all treated as short-term capital gains or ordinary income.

SPYI is great in that it offers investors broadly diversified exposure to some of the world's largest and highest quality companies, including the Magnificent Seven, while still paying out a very attractive income stream by creating a more stable sequence of returns through selling covered calls. On the flip side, as I pointed out in a recent article, this strategy does have the downside of somewhat locking in permanent losses whenever there is a sharp market crash followed by a V-shaped recovery, as the covered calls keep the underlying holdings from bouncing back sharply from losses in their entirety. As a result, it is important to not make this the core part of your portfolio, and so in this article, I would only make it a 33% weighting and have the other two holdings also at 33% weighting.

Portfolio Component #2

The next fund that I would go to is the Reaves Utility Income Trust (UTG), which is a CEF that invests primarily in utilities stocks, as its name indicates. It also holds some broader infrastructure companies on top of that, giving it a bit of exposure into that segment, which does not occupy a large percentage at all of SPYI, further enhancing the diversification of the portfolio. Additionally, the defensive nature of utilities and infrastructure makes it a good holding for a durable passive income stream, as the regulated and contracted nature of utilities and infrastructure assets means that they tend to hold up very well during periods of economic weakness.

This is also evidenced in the fact that UTG has not only sustained its dividend over the long term but has actually grown it.

On top of that, UTG is fairly attractively priced right now as it currently trades at a discount to its net asset value, despite on average trading at a slight premium to its net asset value. It also has a very attractive 7.3% dividend yield, has relatively low leverage below 20%, which further reduces risks during a downturn, and it also has pretty low management fees compared to other actively managed CEFs, such as one of its peer competitors in the Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund (UTF).

Another reason to like UTG is that it has outperformed its broader sector (XLU) over the long term, giving us confidence that the active management running the fund knows what they are doing, and not only generates attractive long-term total returns for shareholders but also sustains and grows the payout accordingly.

This should give retirees a good bit of confidence in the fund manager's ability to responsibly allocate capital and meet the fund's long-term objectives.

Portfolio Component #3

The third and final fund that can meet the goals of a portfolio like this is the InfraCap REIT Preferred ETF (PFFR). It is a pretty simple fund that is diversified across a broad range of REIT (VNQ) preferreds while paying out very attractive monthly dividends that currently come in at around an 8.2% annualized yield.

The beauty of this fund is that it also charges a fairly attractive 0.45% expense ratio and offers investors effectively preferred equity in a large cross-section of institutional, commercial, and residential real estate, which makes it quite a durable source of income. This is because real estate, by nature, tends to be a more stable source of massive income due to the highly contracted and essential nature of real estate. On top of that, a preferred position in the capital stack makes the income even safer as preferred dividends have to be paid out before common dividends are paid out, and the REIT status of these companies means that they have to pay out at least 90% of their taxable income as dividends in order to retain their income tax-exempt status.

Moreover, the real asset nature of REITs makes them more conservative because these companies can always sell off or mortgage individual pieces of real estate (or in the case of a mortgage REIT, their loans are backed by liquid hard assets) to get out of financially challenging situations much more easily than many other business models can. This means that in the case of REIT preferreds, even if the company enters into some operational and/or financial challenges and has to eliminate their common dividends, preferred dividends are cut only in rare circumstances. REITs also generally only very rarely go bankrupt, providing a long-term sense of safety and recoverability to these REIT preferreds. This is also true because even in events where REIT preferred dividends have to be reduced or suspended, the vast majority of them are cumulative in nature, which means that there is a good chance that those dividends will eventually be recovered by shareholders. Given that the fund is so broadly diversified as well across individual holdings, it even further reduces the risks associated with its investment portfolio, since even if one or two of those REITs do have such significant problems that their preferred dividends have to be reduced or eliminated altogether, it will only have a very minor impact on the overall yield of the fund.

When you combine these strengths with the fund's very attractive yield and the goal of this portfolio to generate sustainably high passive income, some of which will be used to meet living expenses and some of which will be used to be reinvested to keep up with the gross effects of inflation, PFFR seems like a really good fit. This is even more the case when you consider the fact that neither SPYI nor UTG have significant real estate exposure, so PFFR nestles in quite nicely into this portfolio, while further accentuating its recession resistance.

Investor Takeaway

When you combine a 33% weighting in SPYI with a 33% weighting in UTG and a 33% weighting in PFFR, you get a roughly 9% weighted average annualized dividend yield. Additionally, all three of these funds pay out their dividends on a monthly basis, so you effectively get a 0.75% yield paid out each month like clockwork. Combining that with the broadly diversified and fairly conservative posture of this portfolio makes it a remarkably simple yet powerful passive income stream, making it potentially a good fit for a retiree's portfolio, though, you should, of course, consult with a personal financial advisor before building your retirement income portfolio. This is the sort of approach that we take, albeit with even more favorable risk-reward, through mostly individual common stock, preferred stock, and baby bond selections in our retirement portfolio at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Bank OZK: Solid Q1 And Poised To Generate Value Amidst Trade Uncertainty
data.description:
Bank OZK overview

Bank OZK (NASDAQ:OZK) has been a mixed performer this year, with shares essentially flat from a year ago. That change masks a significant amount of volatility, as OZK has faced concerns about its commercial real estate (CRE) exposure, while its underlying results have actually been solid. Shares have not been immune to the market sell-off, but the stock jumped 6% on Thursday in response to strong quarterly results. I last covered shares in January, reiterating them as a “strong buy,” but the stock has lost 18% since then given economic fears. After Q1 results, I remain very bullish.

Balance sheet growth remains strong

In the company’s first quarter, Bank OZK earned $1.47, which beat estimates by $0.03 as revenue rose marginally to $409 million. Earnings were down 2.5% from last year, as the company has faced narrowing margins from the Fed’s rate cuts. OZK also continues to invest in growth, aiming to add 34 branches this year. This is driving a 10% increase in non-interest expense in 2025, but as these branches ramp, that should support ongoing deposit growth. With its strong capital position, OZK can take advantage of volatility to grow. Plus, it executed on $35 million of repurchases so far in April, given the share price drop.

OZK remains one of the fastest-growing regional banks I follow. Deposit rose by $900 million sequentially to $31.9 billion. This continues with its trajectory of growth over several years. Deposits are up about $2.5 billion from last year, more than 8%. We did see a 29bp decline in interest-bearing deposit costs in the quarter, given lower short-term interest rates. I expect to see ongoing deposit cost improvement. In Q2, $5.6 billion of deposits matured at 4.56% billion, and in Q3, $7.1 billion matured at 4.4%. It should be able to roll deposits below 4%, helping to reduce interest costs.

Given its deposit growth, OZK has been able to aggressively grow its loan book. It now has $31.1 billion of loans, up $1.1 billion sequentially. While Q1 was strong, OZK is maintaining guidance for mid-to-upper single-digit loan growth this year. Given the economic uncertainty, loan demand could slow, so I believe it prudent to retain guidance rather than assume this elevated pace of growth continues. It also has higher scheduled loan maturities in Q2-Q4, which will be a headwind for growth.

Interest margin pressures are manageable

Now, despite this loan growth, OZK’s $375 million of net interest income (NII) was down about 0.6%. That is because loan yields of 7.79% were down 38bps sequentially and 91bps from last year. Nearly 90% of its loan book is floating rate, and so these move down quickly in response to a lower Fed funds rate, while deposits take more time to roll over. Because of this, its net interest margin (NIM) of 4.31% was down 2bps sequentially and 40bps from last year. If Fed funds remain flat, NIM would rise, but further rate cuts would narrow NIM as loans reprice faster than deposits.

One cushion against a sharply lower fed funds rate is that the majority of loans have floors within 100bps of current levels, which will slow loan yield declines even as deposit yields continue to fall. Additionally, its $3 billion investment portfolio has a 4.03% yield, up from 2.93% a year ago. This was up 76bps sequentially, given the maturities of low-yielding bonds. Another $400 million matures this year at 3.17%, which likely creates positive reinvestment opportunities. Given a growing asset base and ongoing deposit cost cuts, I expect to see NII rise from Q1, even if NIM faces ongoing pressure.

OZK's credit quality remains strong

The bigger concern in the past for OZK has been loan losses given its CRE exposure. 62% of loans are from its real estate specialties group. This is down from a peak of 70%, but still, OZK has one of the higher exposures to the asset class. Its portfolio is well-balanced with multifamily its largest category. Across subsectors, its loan-to-value ratios are very conservative, which should greatly mitigate losses given default.

Of course, in real estate, having an accurate appraised value is critical. Another positive to me is that the vast majority of its exposure was issued in 2022 or later. Almost all of its exposure is post-COVID and much comes well after the Fed began its hiking cycle. These events were headwinds for many CRE asset-type valuations, but they were known when OZK underwrote its loans. It is not as though it is carrying office loans underwritten pre-COVID before hybrid work became a significant factor.

This is why I continue to feel its potential loan losses are manageable. Indeed, actual underwriting results remain strong. It had a modest 25bps of net-charge offs. This is a bit above last year’s 20bp level, but it still remains low. It added $19 million to reserves for $639 million, consistent with its gradual reserve build over the past two years. Reserves assume a greater combined risk of a downside or stagflation scenario than a baseline one. With $214 million of nonperforming assets, OZK has about 3x coverage, which I view as very strong, especially given its loan book is largely secured.

Interestingly, if tariffs lead to a corporate-led economic downturn, commercial real estate may not be the problematic sector for banks that it has been widely expected to be. Instead, banks with more C&I (or business) lending could face higher losses. Of course, commercial real estate will not be immune from slower economic activity, which would dampen lease demand. Still, CRE has less direct tariff exposure than businesses or consumers. If anything, tariffs likely increase replacement costs for buildings, which could slow construction activity. Lower supply may support existing properties.

Capital is excellent, and OZK shares are very attractive

I continue to believe OZK is well-positioned from a credit quality perspective. On top of this, it is very well capitalized. It has an 11.32% CET1 ratio and is not saddled with the large unrealized losses on an outsized securities portfolio like other banks. Its strong capital position enables it to take advantage of opportunities to grow when other banks have to pull back. This is also evident in its ability to step in and buy back stock after a large decline. It also increased its dividend for the 59th quarter and offers a 4.3% dividend yield.

Back in January, I saw at least $6 in EPS and viewed 9–10x earnings as fair given concerns about potential CRE losses. Assuming 2-3 rate cuts, I continue to view OZK as likely to earn about $6.00, assuming $20-30 million of quarterly reserve builds. One risk is that loan growth could be a bit slower if demand slows. I do not expect its net charge-offs to move meaningfully even with slower growth, given the nature of its loan book.

Shares are about 7x earnings, which I view as exceptionally attractive, and OZK is a proverbial “baby thrown out with the bathwater” during this market sell-off. Ongoing opportunistic repurchases may also help to support shares. Its strong capital position also leaves OZK better positioned than peers like Regions (RF) or Ally (ALLY) during a prolonged downturn, in my view. As such, I continue to view OZK as one of the most compelling opportunities in the market. Its strong reaction to Q1 results was justified, and I do expect shares to outperform, with a fair value still $55–60.

data.title: Traders Should Exit Hertz Following Recent Short Squeeze
data.description:
Is it 2020 all over again? We once again have global supply chain disruptions, worries about a potential recession, and traders looking to the government for deals and rate cuts to try to simulate the economy.

And, like in 2020, rental car company Hertz (NYSE:HTZ) appears to be in perilous financial condition. As of earlier this month, the company is fighting with bondholders, and it is hiring advisors to work through a potential capital restructuring program to deal with its more than $6 billion debt load.

In 2020, Hertz infamously filed bankruptcy and then saw its share price skyrocket as meme traders gravitated to Hertz stock for whatever reason. After an initial hundreds of percent rally following bankruptcy, however, reality set back in. Hertz stock traded back down and was ultimately delisted. The Hertz excitement, however, would be a precursor to the meme stock moves that were soon to come in companies such as GameStop (GME) and AMC Entertainment (AMC).

And now, the original meme stock has returned. Hertz shares relisted in 2021 after exiting bankruptcy. Shares traded down throughout the past couple years. But, with well-known investor Bill Ackman disclosing a large position in the company this week, HTZ stock suddenly doubled over the past two trading days:

This move isn't quite as shocking as the rally in 2020. In that prior situation, Hertz had already filed bankruptcy and then the momentum traders stepped in and bid the stock up stratospherically. Here, Hertz is in apparent financial distress, but there are still possible avenues to avoiding an eventual bankruptcy restructuring. In any event, it seems lightning has struck twice in the case of this debt-laden car rental firm finding sudden popularity with traders.

What Ackman Sees In Hertz

In a post on social media website X, formerly Twitter, Pershing Square manager Bill Ackman offered a lengthy rationale for the firm's position in Hertz shares.

Ackman explained that his fund had acquired a 19.8% stake in Hertz through a combination of stock and total return swaps. He describes a company which had lost its way due to ordering too many electric vehicles from Tesla (TSLA). These EVs did not resonate with consumers and also declined in value leading to losses upon resale. Ackman believes, however, that the worst is behind Hertz and that the company can rebound for the following reasons:

  • Better rental car industry profitability
  • Resolution of the Tesla fleet issues
  • Successful turnaround plan
  • Highly-leveraged balance sheet can lead to attractive shareholder returns

Ackman has some notes on industry structure which are interesting but arguably not all that relevant to the near-term investment case. Hertz has a huge pile of debt and needs sharply improved near-term results to move the needle. On that point, however, Ackman sees a big potential catalyst from the tariffs. He wrote that:

"Hertz owns a fleet of over 500,000 vehicles valued at approximately $12 billion. A 10% increase in used car prices would equate to a $1.2 billion gain on its auto assets – equivalent to approximately half of the company’s current market capitalization."

This sounds exciting at face value. However, $1.2 billion makes much less of an impact than it might seem at first glance. The company has more than $6 billion in debt in addition to a chunky stack of other liabilities. Gains on vehicle sales -- should they occur -- are likely to go toward paying down and refinancing debt. I don't believe potential gains against the firm's market capitalization is the most relevant of comparisons. Any gains on car sales are better understood in the context of the firm's far larger total enterprise value. Additionally, I expect that new car prices will rise, potentially considerably, due to the tariffs and largely offset the near-term benefits from higher used car prices through a full economic cycle.

Ackman offers an upside bullish case based on a 2029 valuation that supposes $2 billion of annualized adjusted EBITDA. Given Hertz's struggles with profitability for quite a few years now, I would take 2029 assumptions of robust profitability with a grain of salt. It could happen, but investors can be forgiven for wanting more evidence before accepting this premise.

Finally, Ackman concludes by suggesting that Hertz' fleet of 500,000 vehicles could make it a great partner for Uber (UBER). I grant the possibility that there could be some synergies from a potential partnership. But I wouldn't attach much valuation to this scenario until we got a much more concrete demonstration of a deal and the potential economics from it.

Why I'm Not Buying The Bullish Pitch

In his tweet, Ackman credited the management team with a "successful operational turnaround plan". I just don't see it, at least not in the numbers to date. Here are Hertz' Q4 2024 results:

The company saw worsening stats for most metrics. Q4's silver lining came from slightly higher fleet utilization, but that can be explained by the reduction in fleet size.

Ackman credits the team for successful handling of the fleet adjustment program, for example, yet we see in the commentary that the company is taking higher-than-expected losses on sale. At another point in the commentary, higher insurance and rent expense headwinds are blamed for "masking productivity" at the business.

It's possible that Hertz will become a successful turnaround in the future. That said, the Q4 results don't look like the sort of thing that you typically see when a company has already come out of a prior slump. And, let's not forget, the company was still operating in a fairly strong economic environment in Q4 of 2024.

It's perfectly reasonable to wonder if things such as fleet utilization will go back down in the back half of 2025 as consumers pull back on spending. In particular, we've seen a sizable decline in international tourists arriving to the United States in recent weeks along with airlines such as United Airlines (UAL) offering more cautious guidance for the remainder of the year given the mounting macroeconomic uncertainty.

I'd also suggest there are deeper structural issues at Hertz than just the unfortunate Tesla vehicle order which led to large operating losses and depreciation charges. Take the pandemic period, for example. Publicly-traded peer Avis Budget Group (CAR) made a windfall during that time as used vehicle prices soared and Avis, unlike Hertz, cashed in on the opportunity:

Notably, however, Avis stock has struggled over the past year. Investors had been hoping for a decline in interest rates, which would have made it easier for car rental companies to manage their debts. Also, lower interest rates tend to lead to firmer used car prices, all else held equal. CAR stock has slumped dramatically since the end of 2023 as investors are taking a less optimistic view of the industry's intermediate-term outlook. Even the recent tariff-related catalyst for used car prices hasn't made much of an impact for Avis shares.

This casts into doubt Ackman's contention that the tariffs are going to be a game-changer in terms of Hertz's valuation. Meanwhile, Avis is strongly profitable, has a more proven capital allocation track record, and is selling at less than 10x projected forward earnings even after this week's rally. For investors that are bullish on the car rental stock theme, Avis seems like a much safer choice than the highly-levered and unprofitable Hertz.

Hertz Stock Verdict

I want to qualify my sell rating here. Consider it more of an avoid. For investors that have owned Hertz shares for a while, this recent share price surge makes for an excellent opportunity to get out of that position at a much more attractive level. And for traders that happened to ride alongside Ackman with this move, that's turned out well and this looks like a great time to cash those chips in.

I personally would be shy about outright short selling Hertz shares here. There were already other large institutional holders in Hertz prior to Pershing Square getting involved. Knighthead Capital alone owns nearly 60% of Hertz. This means that the free float is quite small. For example, HTZ stock was not available for short sale for part of the day on Thursday at Interactive Brokers. When that brokerage can't find borrow, it's usually indicative of a highly-shorted stock that is prone to the possibility of sharp short-term price squeezes. With a well-known investor involved and a small free float of stock, Hertz shares could do just about anything in the near-term.

Over the longer-haul, call it the next six or twelve months, I would assign a high probability that Hertz trades back down to $5/share or less. Potentially much less if the company ends up going down a debt restructuring path by then. But the road to get there might be fairly windy, at least over the next month or two. While I am negative of Hertz long-term however, be cautious and prudent in terms of position sizing if playing the short side directly.

Hertz has a ton of debt and was losing large amounts of money even when the economy was strong. Heading into a potential recession, this could further pressure Hertz' already shaky balance sheet. Ackman's involvement may help Hertz refinance debt or issue additional equity to raise funds. Still, risk seems clearly to the downside from Hertz stock following the recent doubling of the share price.

data.title: RTX Q1 Preview: Don't Expect Fireworks (Just Yet)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Tuesday, April 22nd, next week is going to be a big day for the Defense sector with three out of the Big 4 of US defense companies reporting their Q1 earnings for 2025.

RTX Corporation (NYSE:RTX) is one of those three companies slated to report its Q1 CY25 earnings results on Tuesday morning next week. While I expect the company to continue its streak of positive earnings beats in the Q1 report, I strongly believe markets will be closely watching the company’s backlog to look for any significant changes amidst the backdrop of tariffs.

On one hand, RTX’s defense business has been booming for the company, with strong sales visibility and a healthy book-to-bill ratio seen in its defense backlog. On the other hand, RTX’s commercial business is still being haunted by the ghosts of its “powder metal issue” that plagued its GTF (Geared Turbofan) engines.

The key question is if the company can grow its business given the above crosscurrents, especially with supply chains around the world possibly feeling pressure from the volatile nature of tariffs.

Per my analysis below, RTX remains a Hold as it heads into earnings next week.

RTX’s Commercial Business May Continue To Drag

Based on RTX’s full-year results for 2024, the company is now an $80B business, growing at a CAGR of 9.3% since 2020. Throughout these years, RTX’s P&W (Pratt & Whitney) business that designs, manufactures, and services aircraft engines has been a key catalyst for RTX, growing at a CAGR of 13.7%, outpacing the company’s overall consolidated growth pace.

Then came RTX’s infamous powder metal issue, which I suspect is still limiting the growth potential for RTX. Agreed that management navigated the company & its customers quite well through the situation, but the issue still persists in my view, limiting the additional scope of growth for the P&W GTF engine program. This is something I had observed in my previous earnings preview of RTX, where I noted that RTX could be range-bound for a while until this issue is fully cleared.

P&W GTF engines are regularly used in the narrow-body aircraft market, where the company holds a dominant market share. Some of RTX’s large customers, like Airbus (OTCPK:EADSY), have been very vocal about the issues they still face from the CY23 powder metal issues.

Two months ago, Airbus’ management singled out P&W in a call with investors in February when asked to comment on engine suppliers:

No specific comments on the widebodies, and we get from Rolls-Royce (OTCPK:RYCEY) what we need for 2025 and onwards.

There are challenges on engines for our narrow bodies. On the GTF of Pratt & Whitney, we are still limited by the aftermath of the metal powder issues two years ago and the trajectory that was redefined in two waves of negotiation with them.

Emphasis by author.

Airbus did note that P&W is on a positive trajectory, but the support being provided to Airbus has come at the cost of lesser support for new aircraft engines, which still grounds a large number of the Airbus fleet.

Airbus management’s comments on P&W came a day after RTX management struck a more positive tone in a Barclays conference about their progress on providing their customers support on the powder metal issue:

Look, I think that we're not going to wake up on January 1, 2027, and have the entire fleet cleared. But we anticipate being in a much, much more manageable position as a fleet and be able to support our customers and Airbus the way they would expect.

In my view, until RTX management can restore full confidence in its customers about completely putting this powder metal issue behind them, the Commercial business will be sluggish.

The sluggish growth can also be seen in RTX’s Commercial backlog of $125B in CY24, which reported its first full year of mid-single-digit growth last year versus the previous years of low-teens growth.

If the upcoming Q1 CY25 quarter can show RTX’s Commercial backlog picking up pace beyond the 5.9% pace seen last year, that would indicate some early signs about a bottom in the deceleration of the Commercial backlog.

Can Patriot's Contribution To The Defense Backlog Continue?

Contrary to the sluggish growth seen in RTX’s Commercial business, the Defense business is keeping RTX busy.

As seen in Exhibit B above, RTX’s Defense business saw the fastest increase in its backlog last year, growing 25.6% y/y to $9.8B. International strength was one of the main drivers of the backlog. In terms of products, RTX’s Patriot program as well as its F135 program drove many contract wins for RTX last year.

I think RTX has a very good chance of reporting another strong quarter of defense backlog growth based on the contract wins that RTX has already announced through Q1.

RTX started its 2025 with its Patriot product, continuing its strong performance of contract wins. RTX announced two separate Patriot contract wins in Q1—one was the ~$1B contract to supply additional Patriot systems to Romania, while the other was a half-a-billion-dollar contract win where RTX will replenish the Dutch Patriot systems.

Outside of the Patriot program, P&W won a $1.5B contract with the US Air Force in February this year to continue providing maintenance services for its F119 engine that powers the Air Force’s F-22 Raptor stealth fighter jets.

To me, it looks like RTX could see another strong quarter in its Defense backlog.

Above everything, I am very interested in understanding management’s view on the pre-solicitation notice of a massive $50B contract that the DLA (Defense Logistics Agency) may award to the company.

DLA is structuring this contract as a sole-source contract, meaning the winning vendor would provide production systems & support for the Patriot missile defense systems. If & when this contract is awarded, I believe it could be a game changer for RTX. However, I do not expect DLA’s contract to be announced any time soon. This is a mega contract that covers many defense agencies and would require significant planning and coordination on their part. Nevertheless, I will be keenly interested in how management characterizes their view on the DLA notice.

RTX Stock Valuation Premiums Look Tight In A Range

Heading into earnings, RTX’s valuation premium looks to be locked in a tight range with no room for expansion or contraction.

Comparing RTX to itself, I see that the company trades at a forward valuation of ~21x, which is ~9% from the ~23x it traded at last year. So if markets were to test RTX’s valuation premium expansion case, RTX’s stock price could reach $140, which is close to the average target price of $144.

But if optimism were to fall in RTX after its Q1 earnings results, I believe markets can re-evaluate RTX’s average valuation premium of ~19x. The contraction in premium implies a 9-10% drop in the stock price, bringing it down to the ~$116 area.

So I am expecting a +9 to -9% move in RTX’s stock post the Q1 earnings results unless there is a significant catalyst that management announces on the call.

What To Look For In RTX's Q1 Earnings Report

Here are some of the estimates investors should look out for in the Q1 report:

  • Q1 consensus estimates: EPS of $1.37 on revenues of $19.8B, growing 2.6% y/y.
  • Full-year CY25 consensus estimates: $6.11 EPS on revenues of $84.2B, growing 4.3% y/y. Both these estimates are above management’s midpoint projections of $6.8 EPS on revenues of $83.5B, growing 3.4% y/y.
  • Management commentary on backlog, international business, impacts of tariffs, and supply chain health.

On tariffs, management noted in the Barclays conference that the company is much better positioned this time around, with their supply chain more diversified worldwide. In that February conference, management believed they were “well positioned as we head in here in 2025,” with respect to tariffs.

Takeaway

RTX Corp. looks steady as it heads into the earnings report next week. Weakness in its Commercial business looks to be offset by strength in its Defense business. The performance of these two businesses will mostly continue in a similar way in Q1 as well, in my view, unless significant catalysts can change the outlook for RTX.

RTX continues to warrant a Hold rating in my view.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: How Tariff Troubles May Hurt Europe's Growth
data.description:
President Trump’s tariffs bring déjà vu for the euro-area economy: its back to slower growth and lower rates.

Markets rallied on the announcement of President Trump’s 90-day pause in his administration’s new global trade tariffs - with notable exceptions:

  • The moratorium was restricted to trade partners that haven’t retaliated.
  • Reciprocal tariffs weren’t lifted but instead lowered to 10%, except for China, which is now subject to a 125% levy.
  • The 25% global tariff on steel, aluminum and automobile imports remains in force.

As well as the actual impact of the tariff measures, business confidence has also taken a hit, with survey evidence already pointing to lower economic growth. Further tariff-related initiatives, such as the US inquiry into imports of pharmaceutical goods and semiconductors, will likely create more uncertainty and loss of confidence.

Tariffs Are Already Damaging Europe’s Growth

Instead of the previously announced 20% tariff, the European Union (EU) is now subject to a 10% universal tariff - still a higher effective tariff rate than prior to the April 2 US announcement. Meanwhile, the EU voted on April 9 to impose tariffs on €21 billion of US goods - countermeasures that have also been put on hold. Despite the tariff pauses, the danger for euro-area economies is far from over, and damage is already being done.

Based on our assessment, growth is set to fall, with risks skewed to the downside. Compared with a counterfactual scenario in which the new tariffs were never implemented, and with only marginal damage to confidence, the hit to growth will probably be substantial. In the Display below, we show our 2025 forecasts across different scenarios. The wide range of possible outcomes reflects the high level of tariff uncertainty.

Tariff Uncertainty: The Range of Potential Outcomes Is Very Wide

Comparative Forecast Scenarios for 2025 Growth, Inflation and Rates (Percent)

With the economic recovery still only recent and weak, the euro area is once again likely to face low growth or recession risks, partly from tariff impacts and partly from uncertainty and loss of confidence.

Risks of Inflation Undershoot Are Rising

The eurozone was already heading towards its target 2% inflation on a sustainable basis during the course of this year. However, with growth now expected to weaken and the economy potentially to enter recession, the downward pressure on inflation is likely to intensify, aided by three additional factors:

  • Energy prices are a major driver of inflation: they fell significantly following the April 2 US tariff announcement and remain relatively depressed.
  • Financial conditions in the US have been tightening, and the euro area could experience the initial knock-on effects. Meanwhile, the euro has strengthened against the US dollar, making US imports cheaper. If the euro remains strong, this effect will continue.
  • China will continue to seek markets for its exports because tariff barriers make exporting to the US uneconomic. The country will likely reroute its goods to other regions, notably the EU. This was the case during Trump’s first round of tariffs, when exports to the EU surged (Display, below). The reaction is likely to be greater this time around.

More Chinese Exports May Be Heading Europe’s Way

Previous US Tariffs Led to Rerouting of China Exports

Hence, our baseline forecast is that the European Central Bank will cut policy rates to below 2%, reaching 1.75% by the end of December. The probability of more cuts increases in line with recession and undershooting risks.

No Easy Solutions from Fiscal Policy

Fiscal policy is unlikely to be as supportive as it was during the energy crisis, as most EU member states face tight budget constraints.

Some countries might provide sector-specific relief, like the €14 billion package the Spanish government announced recently. But only Germany has the headroom to provide material fiscal support. Given that Germany could be facing a third year of recession, it’s possible that fiscal restrictions will be suspended to provide immediate support to its industries.

Even so, the EU as a whole faces a tough position.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations and do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams. Views are subject to revision over time.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Netflix's Next Act: From Hyperscale Growth To Ad-Driven Margin Expansion
data.description:
Netflix is growing up

Netflix (NASDAQ:NFLX) is up after hours after a solid earnings print. I last covered Netflix in November of 2024, just after the Tyson Vs Paul fight. The event left me less than impressed as millions tuned in to watch the spectacle. Watching the program in real time was nearly un-watchableand most had to watch a couple minutes before live to allow for the program to buffer. Better results in live stream during the Christmas NFL spread was a good follow-up to get their live game back on track.

I'm starting to come around to Netflix. As a streamer, they're clearly the leader and the path to profit growth and margin expansion is becoming clearer post-earnings.Let's take a look at the tape.

Netflix Q1 earnings results

After the “leak” about how Netflix intends to grow to a trillion-dollar market cap by 2030 through increased ad revenue and user growth, the market was primed with high expectations for this earnings print. Revenue beat slightly and earnings per share beat by a lot [$6.61 v $5.71 expected], that was a whopping 15.76% beat.

This is the first quarter without subscriber growth data. Operating efficiency is becoming the focus along with margin expansion as ad revenue is layered on. Price hikes across their loyal consumer base are now going to be a trend as hyper-scaling mode may be maxed outand monetizing the base is now paramount. So far so good with the after-market pop today. The market will still be trying to find subscriber growth through the rumor mill. For now, this looks like a buyable momentum story and less prone to trade war shock than other mega-cap names.

Let's look at some important commentary from the earnings call transcript:

Now, we think we got a pretty good business today, over $40 billion in revenue. We've got over 300 million paid households. Those represent an audience of over 700 million individuals. We're leading in streaming view share.

But we also think that we're a minority of our addressable market -- our potential across any of those measures. You think about engagement, we're less than 10% of TV hours from an audience or a connected households perspective. We still got hundreds of millions of folks to sign-up, and from a revenue perspective, we're about 6% of consumer spend in ad revenue in the countries we serve, in the areas that we serve.

So, we believe we've got plenty of room to grow our engagement, our revenue, and our profit. And as Ted said, do that to become the most valued and loved entertainment company for all of our stakeholders.

The big switch

So as you can see, the talking points are now pivoting to ad revenue as a primary driver of forward profit and free cash flow growth. The company has an $8 billion target for 2025, which is great progress from an operational standpoint for a company that was once free cash flow negative during the fight to gain streaming dominance, mainly pre-COVID:

The company also reiterated continued share buybacks and further self-funding reinvestment back into the business which of course includes the popular content that slowly has Netflix becoming the new Hollywood studios.

Let's take a look at the company balance sheet management as it relates to shares outstanding, EBITDA, debt and cash equivalents:

The company sports a very healthy balance sheet. Long-term debt is only 1.6 X the company's cash and short-term investments and the company is only borrowing 59% of its EBITDA. Most companies can borrow up to 3 X their EBITDA if needed. Shares outstanding were on an upward trajectory but the company is now returning capital to shareholders in the form of buybacks being that they are free cash flow positive and growing. All in all, Netflix runs a pretty conservative balance sheet. Also, media companies have the benefit of a lot of amortization of goodwill from intangible media content.

Netflix also has an advantage over a Disney (DIS) or Comcast (CMCSA), they don't have theme parks to manage. These parks [the ones in China] have been threatened as some sort of token or target in the trade war with China. Nor do they have to deal with legacy cable channels or the actual service like Disney and Comcast do. As a pure streamer, Netflix can remain focused squarely on pulling ahead in the streaming race without a lot of other headaches.

NFLX stock is still expensive

Charlie Munger once said, " Everybody with any sense at all knows that some companies are better than others. What makes it difficult is they sell at higher prices in relation to assets, earnings, and so forth, and that takes the fun out of the game." Same case here with Netflix, they've been crowned the top streamer, earnings are growing, TAM is growing and the shift from traditional cable packages to streaming packages is happening right now. The market loves Netflix, thus it's high momentum at the moment and it's hard to stop the elevator.

Investors are desperately looking for names that can avoid tariffs. The fewer physical products you ship overseas or import the better. Netflix has no moving parts floating on ships. They look pretty tariff-proof.

Growth expectations

Well, this is about as "up and to the right" a trajectory as you'll find in the market for analyst estimates. It might just be one of those stocks you have to plug your nose and say, "Well, at least I'm only paying 16 X 2030 earnings". With the release of each earnings report that beats expectations, more and more investors are going to do just that. We basically have low 20% to high teens earnings growth expected every year from now until 2033.

Furthermore, because of how recently and dramatically Netflix has increased all profitability metrics, the price growth over the last 5 years still sits below the growth rate in free cash flow and EPS growth. This is actually a pretty encouraging chart for an expensive company. Reminds me a bit of the NVIDIA lift-off that few saw coming [myself included].

Risks and summary

Earnings were a hit, beating EPS and showing that the focus is now profit and free cash flow above chasing subscribers as the primary goal. The content is there and streaming is becoming the new normal. Still, buying here is very risky due to the expensive multiple. Disney+ is a contender, but not yet a viable threat. The target of a trillion-dollar market cap is also pushing all the right analyst buttons, the buzz is everywhere on the airwaves. Sometimes you have to wonder about "leaks". Hopefully, Netflix can continue to meet market expectations.

I am assigning this a "spec-buy" rating, an upgrade from the previous hold. I am impressed by their growth in all levels of profitability and I do believe streaming will replace all cable package type of viewership sooner than later. I don't own Netflix yet, I have owned it in the past [when Bill Ackman entered] and exited when Ackman did too which I feel silly about now. It was the same level of regret of following Buffett in and out of Walmart. In short, Netflix continues to deliver and this is one of the few names that shouldn't be lowering guidance regardless of a recession or tariffs.

data.title: TSMC: Extremely Bullish On Earnings
data.description:
Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (NYSE:TSM) (OTC:TSMWF) stock has lost 23% since I initiated coverage in January with a strong buy rating. In this report, I am revisiting the company and discussing the most recent results. Additionally, I will discuss the risks, explain why I believe the stock is still a Strong Buy and update my price target.

TSMC Sees Strong Growth Driven By High-Performance Computing Sales

First-quarter sales grew 35% to $25.5 billion and exceeded the midpoint of the guided range, and that was despite an earthquake causing some lost production in January. Gross margins increased by 5.7 points and were at the high end of the guided range, while operating margins of 48.5% expanded by 6.5 points and met the high end of the guided range. This resulted in net profit margins increasing 5.1 points to 43.1%.

The gross margins declined sequentially, driven by dilutive impact from the Arizona fab and the earthquake impact.

By technology, we see that 7nm and 5nm revenues have remained stable sequentially. Revenues for 3nm technology were lower and that is primarily driven by the lower smartphone end-market sales in line with seasonality. Year-on-year, we do see higher sales for 5nm technology and substantial growth in 3nm technology revenues.

By the end market, smartphone and Internet of Things sales were down sequentially, but automotive, high-performance computing and consumer electronics were up. Growth in HPC continued and accounted for nearly 60% of the sales driven by AI.

What Are The Risks And Opportunities For TSMC?

For TSM, the opportunities seem to be rather clear. There is demand for chips driven by more chip implementations in products, demand for advanced chips, and continued growth in AI applications. With its 3nm technology, the company is positioned well to capture demand for advanced and energy-efficient chips. The company is working on 2nm technology, which will enter in the second half of the year built on 2nm node technology, and for 2026 the company is working on N2P, which will deliver even more efficient and powerful chips. So, TSMC is already a market leader and it is further strengthening that position.

There are also risks. One risk that we did see in the first quarter is the risk of natural disasters that may affect production. Furthermore, the current trade turmoil may reduce economic growth and thereby demand, and tighter export controls may affect TSMC. However, a positive is that the company has seen no change in customer behaviour yet:

Now let me talk about the recent tariff. We understand there are uncertainties and risks from the potential impact of tariff policies. However, we have not seen any change in our customers' behavior so far. Therefore, we continue to expect our full-year 2025 revenue to increase by close to mid-20 percent in the U.S. dollar term. We might get a better picture in the next few months, and we will continue to closely monitor the potential impact on the end market demand and manage our business prudently. Amidst those uncertainties, we continue to focus on the fundamentals of our business with our technology leadership, manufacturing excellence, and customer trust to further strengthen our competitive position. As such, we are confident TSMC can continue to outperform the Foundry 2.0 industry growth in 2025.

There might be a change and there might be impacts from tariffs, but for now, it is good to see that the company is maintaining its outlook for 2025. What we do see, however, is a growing dilution of gross margins. In the second quarter, the company expects an 80-basis points headwind on margins due to the dilutive nature of the Arizona fab with a two- to three-point headwind for the full year, and over the longer term this is expected to grow to a three- to four-point headwind due to cost inflation and tariffs.

We see a lot of talk about opening fabs in the US, but the reality is that due to a combination of higher labor costs, higher construction costs, and a supply chain that is less integrated on the local level, there is a margin headwind. However, I believe that geographic diversification to grow sales is favourable even if that comes at a small dilution of margins.

TSM Stock Remains A Strong Buy

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

I believe that TSM remains a strong buy; there could be some risks due to tariffs, but the company has not seen any change in customer behaviour nor have analyst estimates come down dramatically. We are looking at a 0.5% decline between 2025 and 2027, which still aligns the company for booking nearly 20% in annual EBITDA growth.

Free cash flow estimates have come down a bit more at 8%, but that still points to strong 25.8% annual growth in EBITDA, and we see that the pressure for EBITDA and FCF is mostly in 2026.

With the down revision primarily on FCF and the lower peer group multiple, I am now targeting a $233.2 price for TSM, providing a 54% upside.

Conclusion: TSMC Remains A Strong Buy Despite Risks

I do believe that TSMC is definitely facing some risks and while I am not expecting a major impact in 2025, there could be more pressure in 2026 if trade turmoil does not fade. However, the reality is that more than likely, TSMC remains an innovative leader that will position the company for strong growth in the years ahead. As a result, I remain extremely bullish on the stock.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Ultrapar: The Worst Is Over
data.description:
It's been a year since my last article about Ultrapar (NYSE:UGP) and I'm glad my analyses proved meaningful. Due to its significant appreciation during early 2024, I shared my belief that all good news was already priced in and market-beating returns were unlikely. Since then, UGP has seen its price drop by almost 50% while the S&P500 is unchanged.

If this is the first time you read about Ultrapar, I'd recommend you read my second article, as it has the reasons why I was bullish and a deep dive into smaller units of the company. This article will focus on giving an update on the business and why I believe it deserves a Hold Rating.

This change is driven more by my expectation that both valuation and exchange rate have hit rock-bottom than any significant improvement in the financials that make it a Buy.

Ipiranga's Improvement Has Stalled, But It's Better Than the Recent Past

The main argument I made for Ultrapar price appreciation was the expectation that its Gross Margin would significantly improve following Petrobras' (PBR) new price policy, which was basically in sharp contrast to the old PPI (Preço de Paridade de Importação, in Portuguese). PPI used to price gasoline based on both international oil prices and the USD/BRL exchange rate, while the new price policy simply says that Petrobras will take into consideration other variables (i.e., political ones) to decide on price.

In Q3 '23 and Q4 '23, the company had two strong quarters for Gross Margin in the Ipiranga business, achieving 7.1% and 7.7%, respectively. That was what excited investors and supported the price rally until February 2024. However, the Gross Margin for the entire 2023 was 5.7%, and in 2024 it was 5.4%. Since Gross Margin improvement seems to have stalled, investors sold the stock as the price appreciation was unjustified for a ~5% Gross Margin.

So, although the new price policy from Petrobras was an improvement for Ipiranga's margins, it wasn't enough to support recovery towards the 2016 level. Even though crude oil prices have stabilized and have even inched lower in recent months, the USD/BRL exchange rate suffered a 20% devaluation during 2024, which made Petrobras increase its prices—not as volatile as it was under PPI, but still enough to cap gains for Ipiranga.

One bull argument could be that if the USD/BRL exchange rate settles or improves and crude oil prices go down with fears of a global recession, that could support Petrobras to keep prices steady or even lower, supporting, in turn, an improvement in Ipiranga's margins. However, at this moment, our best estimate is likely a ~5% Gross Margin.

Ultragaz and Ultracargo Continue To Improve Performance and Stabilize Ultra's Earnings

As mentioned in my second article, Ultragaz and Ultracargo, although representing less than 10% of Ultra's Revenue, now account for 1/3 of EBITDA and almost 40% of Operating Profit. This is important because these businesses are much more stable than gas stations. Also, Ultracargo has multiplied by more than 5x its Operating Profit since 2019, while Ultragaz tripled it since 2021.

Both are businesses with low organic growth, usually depending on acquisitions to really move the needle. If Ultrapar focuses on sensible M&As that aren't overpriced, they may be able to increase further the share of these businesses compared to Ipiranga. More Operating Profit from Ultracargo and Ultragaz would, in my opinion, make Ultrapar command higher multiples due to its stability.

Funding M&A with Debt

One other main argument from my first article was that the significant leverage the company achieved in the late 2010s was being fixed due to the sale of Oxiteno and Extrafarma. This process was largely finalized during 2023, when the company reported an EBITDA to Net Debt close to 1.0 and a total Debt of R$11.8B, the lowest since 2016.

However, even with this improvement, Ultrapar's interest payments as a percentage of Revenue have increased to ~1.5% since 2022 compared to ~1.0% right before the pandemic. This is mainly driven by an increase in Interest Rates since 2022 which continues to this date, achieving 14.25% in April versus 5%~6% right before COVID.

It doesn't help Ultrapar's case the fact that they increased debt in 2024 by R$2.5B largely to fund the acquisition of Hidrovias do Brasil. They acquired a highly leveraged company with little to no profit, and they paid an average of ~3.50BRL per share. However, it now trades at ~2.60BRL but traded close to 2.0BRL recently.

To gain back those 0.5% in Net Income that were eaten away by higher interest rates, Ultrapar would have to decrease its debt by R$1.5B when it was at R$11.8B, not increase by R$2.5B due to an acquisition that currently doesn't contribute to earnings. Hopefully, the fact that the stock has been hammered throughout 2024 pushes the company to change course on this strategic decision.

Valuation

My updated valuation will be simpler than the DCF models I shared in previous articles. Although those models continue to be valid, if you check them, you need to consider that the Interest Rate in Brazil is at 14.25% and is expected to continue at this level throughout 2025. If all goes well, it's expected to go down during 2026, so the Present Value in those DCFs should be ~14% instead of the ~10% I guided two years ago.

With that said, it's obvious that stocks are likely to benefit once interest rates start to go down. The Present Value with a ~14% Discount Rate in the DCFs gives us a valuation similar to where it is now. Another way to see how undervalued Ultrapar has become is through the Price Earnings Ratio below. Note that trailing 7.5 PE is well below its historical trend.

In the end, Ultrapar revaluation depends on two factors. First, if Ipiranga can continue to improve its margins. This could come either through operational efficiency or through Petrobras' lower gasoline prices in case Crude Oil prices or the USD/BRL exchange rate help. I don't believe any of these possibilities are strong enough to recommend a Buy, but they don't seem to have much more room to fall either.

Ultracargo and Ultragaz will continue to provide stable earnings and cash flow, and hopefully, the company won't engage in further M&As without taking into consideration the Debt level and how they can really derive value from the acquisition.

Final Remarks and Risks

I don't believe significant Risks are on the horizon for Ultrapar due to its already depressed valuation. However, recession is a strong possibility for the world economy and Brazil would also be impacted by it. More volatility in the USD/BRL exchange rate or more interest rate tightening are two risks I could also point out.

Investors who are tempted to invest again in Ultrapar should keep a close eye on Ipiranga's gross margin and the USD/BRL exchange rate to try to anticipate any improvements in earnings. At this moment, I don't see any clear catalyst that makes Ultrapar a Buy, but I also don't see any significant downside risk.

data.title: Gauging The Fear Factor: From Volatility Peaks To Equity Returns
data.description:
From Crisis to Recovery: Global and US Equity Returns After VIX Peaks

Stock markets have been rattled by trade war tensions and economic uncertainty driven by US tariff policies. Yet history suggests that equities have usually performed well in the aftermath of peak market volatility.Investors have been coping with acute market swings in recent weeks. Global and US stocks tumbled in the days after US President Donald Trump unveiled his “Liberation Day” tariffs on April 2. Then we saw a dramatic rebound when a 90-day reprieve on most tariffs was announced on April 9, followed by exemptions for electronic equipment such as smartphones and computers. More volatility is likely in the coming weeks while trade policy remains so fluid.Moments like these are understandably tough for investors to stomach. The pain of severe downturns makes it hard to stay the course, even in the most well-designed long-term investment plan. At the same time, withdrawing from equity markets during steep declines risks locking in losses and forfeiting recovery potential.

How Have Stocks Performed After Very Scary Moments?

History can offer a helpful perspective. It may sound counterintuitive, but during the past quarter century, peak market volatility in very serious crises often gave way to powerful equity market returns in the subsequent 12 months (Display). In months when the VIX Index, an index of US equity market volatility, also known as the fear index, reached between 40 and 50, returns for the MSCI World and S&P 500 averaged 37.4% and 34.4%, respectively, over the next 12 months. And when the VIX breached 50, returns for US and global stocks were also very strong over the following year. Emerging-market equities have also done quite well after VIX peaks. On April 8, the VIX hit 52.3, the highest since the pandemic in March 2020.Some caution is warranted in this analysis. Extreme VIX readings are very uncommon; the index only exceeded 40 nine times at month-end during the 24-year period surveyed above. Of course, past performance doesn’t guarantee future results. And today’s macro and market conditions are unprecedented in many ways. The range of outcomes for economic growth and corporate earnings is especially wide and hard to forecast with so much riding on unpredictable policy decisions.That said, past volatility peaks also came at very scary market moments. The global financial crisis of 2008–2009, the euro debt crisis in 2011 and the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020 are etched into investors’ collective memories as some of the most terrifying times in modern financial history.

Staying Invested Is a Strategic Imperative

Nobody knows how the trade war will evolve from here. Peak volatility tends to forebode the worst-case scenarios. But if things turn out better than expected, volatility may ultimately subside and better outcomes than feared can be achieved.Since it’s nearly impossible to time market inflection points, we believe that staying invested in equities is a strategic imperative. In our view, portfolios that focus on company fundamentals can target the quality businesses best positioned to weather trade war challenges and thrive over the long term. It’s also important to deploy strategic and tactical risk-management tools that are suited to the current challenges. Diversified allocations based on disciplined investing processes can provide investors with the confidence to stay in the market through uncertain times—and to benefit from the long-term return potential that often materializes when markets shift from fear to hope.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations, and do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams and are subject to change over time.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
